---
title: "Get started"
subtitle: "From event timing to a typed first-level fit."
jupyter: python3
---

## Install

```bash
pip install fmrimod
```

For local development from this checkout:

```bash
pip install -e .
```

## The 30-Second Tour

Start with an event table and a sampling frame. The event table stores
experimental timing; the sampling frame defines the acquired time grid. In
the full workflow, those timing objects travel with an `fmri_dataset` into
`fmri_lm()`.

```{python}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import fmrimod as fm
from fmrimod.design_colmap import design_colmap

rng = np.random.default_rng(7)
events = pd.DataFrame({
    "onset": [0, 12, 24, 36],
    "condition": ["face", "scene", "face", "scene"],
    "duration": [2, 2, 2, 2],
})

sf = fm.SamplingFrame(blocklens=40, TR=1.0)
data = rng.normal(size=(40, 8))
dataset = fm.fmri_dataset(data, tr=1.0, events=events)
```

Use a formula to turn those events into typed task regressors. The string
formula is convenience syntax for the same design-spec tree used by the
object builders.

```{python}
model = fm.event_model(
    'onset ~ hrf(condition, basis="spmg1")',
    data=events,
    sampling_frame=sf,
)

model.column_names
```

The resulting matrix has one row per acquired sample and one column per model
regressor. It is inspectable before fitting.

```{python}
design = model.design_matrix
design.shape
```

Fit through the canonical first-level seam. The result carries coefficient
matrices, residual degrees of freedom, solver configuration, and contrast
methods.

```{python}
fit = fm.fmri_lm("hrf(condition)", dataset)
fit.n_coefficients, fit.n_voxels
```

## What Just Happened

`SamplingFrame` defines the time axis, `fmri_dataset()` binds data to timing
and events, `event_model()` shows the compiled design surface, and
`fmri_lm()` fits the public first-level path. This is the same architectural
shape used by flagship parity workflows:

```text
fmri_dataset -> fmri_lm -> contrast -> group_fit
```

You can inspect the design surface with the same top-level API:

```{python}
colmap = design_colmap(model)
colmap[["col", "name", "role", "model_source"]]
```

## Next Steps

- [Tutorial: design matrices](tutorials/design-matrices.qmd)
- [Tutorial: linear model](tutorials/linear-model.qmd)
- [Tutorial: group analysis](tutorials/group-analysis.qmd)
- [API reference](reference/index.qmd)
